learning_outcomes.description: Analyse science topics, identifying appropriate levels of conceptual development and contexts for teaching the topics in the primary years of schooling.
Use science education literature to inform your teaching practice.
Collaboratively develop resources including activities, laboratory experiments and units of work for teaching using a variety of pedagogical approaches focusing on inquiry learning.
Develop students scientific literacy and ability to participate in scientific thinking in social and technological contexts.
Create pieces of assessment for diagnostic, formative and summative assessment in the science classroom that align with the Australian Curriculum.
Discuss current issues related to assessment of student work in science.

Work effectively and collaboratively in teams to make informed team decisions, demonstrate interpersonal, leadership and communication skills, and critically evaluate personal and peer contributions to team processes.

assessments.task_description:
All students are expected to complete a safety declaration in order to work in the laboratory. The university requires this declaration to be current within 5 years. Records are kept on the OHS database. Laboratory conditions for education are considered to be any space where scientific activity is taking place. Students must be aware that this also translates to primary classrooms where safety is paramount and risk assessment is critical.

As this assessment pertains to OHS, please contact the Course Coordinator directly regarding potential extensions for this assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.assessment_title: Evaluating an Inquiry Unit of Work
assessments.task_description:
A unit plan organises curriculum content into a coherent sequence for learning and teaching over a set period of time (e.g., a term). In Primary Science, the resources we select should support this coherence by meaningfully supporting student engagement and understanding.

In this task, you will analyse an existing unit of work using a CoRe Matrix and evaluate it against the Australian Curriculum: Science V9 through the lens of Constructive Alignment, which focuses on the coherence between learning outcomes, teaching strategies, and assessment.

The task consists of two parts.

Part 1 – Analysis (approx. 1000 words)

Critique a Primary Connections unit of work and use this analysis to develop a CoRe Matrix for the three phases of the unit (Launch, Inquire, Act). The CoRe matrix provides a holistic representation of a sequential unit of work. It helps identify students’ prior knowledge, potential misconceptions, and effective teaching strategies and assessment approaches that promote student learning. [The Matrix template will be provided and modelled in class.]

Building on your critique and through your CoRe matrix, demonstrate a clear understanding of the unit’s conceptual focus and pedagogical intent. This includes articulating an Essential Question for the unit and outlining the background knowledge necessary for effective teaching. Any text taken directly from the Primary Connections resource must be written in italics and appropriately referenced.

Part 2 – Evaluation (approx. 1000 words)

In this section, you will extend your analysis by evaluating the unit through the lens of Constructive Alignment. Your task is to consider both how well the unit aligns learning outcomes, teaching strategies, and assessment, and why this alignment is essential for designing meaningful Primary Science learning.

Your discussion should focus on the significance of alignment for coherent planning: how clear intentions guide purposeful teaching, how aligned learning experiences support conceptual development, and how assessment provides meaningful evidence of learning. You should also reflect on what engaging with Constructive Alignment reveals about your own developing practice and how it shapes your approach to designing intentional, responsive science learning. 

APST: 2.2, 2.3, 3.1, 3.2, 3.4, 3.6, 5.1

If you use AI and/or machine translation (MT) technologies in any way in your assessment task, you must provide specific details about how it was used. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute academic misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Guidelines about conventions for appropriate referencing and acknowledgement, including an example coversheet to provide details of use, are available here: Overview - AI tools for assignments - Library Guides at University of Queensland Library
assessments.submission_guidelines: Please use the Turnitin Submission link on Blackboard to upload your task as one combined Word document (CoRe Matrix table and reflection). Ideally, 11pt font with 1.5 line spacing.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: Plan a Sequence of Learning about a Science Phenomenon
assessments.task_description:
A phenomenon is simply an observable event that can be explained scientifically. In the science classroom, a carefully chosen phenomenon can drive student inquiry. Phenomena add relevance to the science classroom, showing students science in their own world. A good phenomenon is observable, interesting, complex, and aligned to the appropriate curriculum standard.

In a group of three (3), choose a science phenomenon as the focus for your planning:

  • Identify, describe and explain the science behind your phenomenon and its role in the science classroom. [Images may be used]. A synopsis of background scientific information helps teachers deepen their understanding of the science they are required to teach. This should answer the question: As the teacher, what science content do I need to know? (approx. 600 words)
  • Using the identified phenomenon as a starting point, write a sequence of 3 inquiry-based lessons (Launch, Inquire, Act) to support inclusive participation and engagement in the inquiry process. Risk assessment/s and specifics about the classroom environment must be included. Each lesson plan must be attributed to an individual. (approx. 2400 words)


A note about group work:

You will receive a single group mark for this task, as all group members should contribute equally to the design and delivery of the assessment task. However, exceptions can be made in the event of one or more group members engaging in ‘social loafing’ (also known as ‘free-riding’):

Free riding is a form of social loafing observed in groups when one or more members slack off and ‘ride’ on the extra efforts of their co-workers (Walker & Angelo, 1998).

If your small group is experiencing dysfunction, including ‘social loafing’ by one or more members, it is your responsibility to bring this promptly to the attention of your tutor/course coordinator, who will then support the group to resolve the problems in a respectful way. If no resolution can be found, the tutor/course coordinator can take steps, including removing the non-contributing student(s) from the group and requiring the student(s) to complete the task either individually or in another small group with other ‘social loafers’.

APST: 1.2, 2.1, 2.2, 2.5, 2.6, 3.1, 3.2, 3.3, 4.1, 4.4

If you use AI and/or machine translation (MT) technologies in any way in your assessment task, you must provide specific details about how it was used. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute academic misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Guidelines about conventions for appropriate referencing and acknowledgement, including an example coversheet to provide details of use, are available here: Overview - AI tools for assignments - Library Guides at University of Queensland Library

assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 1 grade for each 24 hour period from time submission is due will apply for up to 7 days. After 7 days you will receive a mark of 0.

assessments.task_description:
Working in your groups, you will collaboratively design and deliver a 30-minute LIA (Launch-Inquire-Act) micro-teaching episode that teaches a science concept based on your identified phenomenon. Your presentation should model inquiry-based science teaching and demonstrate how the phenomenon can be used as an anchor for conceptual development.

Your presentation must:

  • Introduce the lesson segment by briefly explaining where your phase fits within the full LIA sequence and how the task(s) in your segment backmap to the intended summative assessment.
  • Teach your allocated phase of the lesson, demonstrating clear pedagogical intent, accurate science content, and strategies that support inclusive participation and engagement.
  • Use a range of verbal and non‑verbal communication strategies to enhance clarity, support inquiry, and promote student understanding.
  • Address safety and classroom environment considerations, including any required risk assessments relevant to your phase.
  • Respond to questions without notice from peers and your tutor, demonstrating your understanding of the science concept, the inquiry process, and your pedagogical choices.

The focus of this task is on developing Pedagogical Content Knowledge, modelling inquiry through the LIA structure, and demonstrating how direct teaching can be used purposefully within an inquiry‑based approach.

The micro-teaching episode will be video-recorded for your reflection (Assessment task 4B) and will occur during class time (lectures or tutorials) in Week 13.

APST: 1.2, 2.2, 3.1, 3.3, 3.4, 3.5
assessments.submission_guidelines: Presentation will occur during class time (lectures or tutorials) in Week 13.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: Late submission of assessment without approved extension request via myUQ system will result in a failing grade.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
After your teaching event, you will receive specific feedback from several of your peers. You will also have access to the video recording of your presentation.

Write an individual critical reflection on your mini‑lesson, supported by relevant literature. Your reflection should:

  • review your own teaching,
  • acknowledge how collaboration with peers (including classroom discussion) contributed to your learning
  • critically examine the strengths and tensions of inquiry‑based learning
  • consider how this professional learning experience informs your development toward becoming a highly proficient science teacher.

APST: 1.2, 2.2, 3.1, 3.3, 3.4, 3.5, 6.1, 6.2

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 1 To Week 3(27 Jul - 16 Aug)
learning_activities.topic:
Teaching Science

The introductory module will provide an overview of the importance and challenges of teaching science to primary-age students in an increasingly STEM-oriented world. Models of curriculum, pedagogy and assessment will be explored in advance of first-hand experience in schools. Tutorials will focus on practical activities designed to engage and motivate young students across the sub-strands of Science Understanding.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04, L05, L06
learning_activities.topic_description: Teaching Science The introductory module will provide an overview of the importance and challenges of teaching science to primary-age students in an increasingly STEM-oriented world. Models of curriculum, pedagogy and assessment will be explored in advance of first-hand experience in schools. Tutorials will focus on practical activities designed to engage and motivate young students across the sub-strands of Science Understanding.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 4 To Week 8(17 Aug - 20 Sep)
learning_activities.topic:
Break due to professional experience placement

No classes scheduled while undertaking professional experience (placement) in an accompanying course.
learning_activities.topic_description: Break due to professional experience placement No classes scheduled while undertaking professional experience (placement) in an accompanying course.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 9 To Week 10(21 Sep - 11 Oct)
learning_activities.topic:
Planning for Teaching Science

After seeing science teaching in the classroom, students will examine critical aspects of planning using science content knowledge and pedagogical content knowledge in order to develop their identity as science teachers. Tutorials will explore the sub-strands of the Science Understanding strand to incorporate content and strategies that promote meaningful and valued learning.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07
learning_activities.topic_description: Planning for Teaching Science After seeing science teaching in the classroom, students will examine critical aspects of planning using science content knowledge and pedagogical content knowledge in order to develop their identity as science teachers. Tutorials will explore the sub-strands of the Science Understanding strand to incorporate content and strategies that promote meaningful and valued learning.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 11 To Week 13(12 Oct - 01 Nov)
learning_activities.topic:
Preparing to Teach Science

Lectures will continue to focus on developing the pedagogical content knowledge and curriculum connections required in teaching science to students in the primary years. Tutorials will allow students to develop their pedagogical skills by practising teaching and evaluating a short lesson around a particular concept. This module also includes opportunity for students to practice through micro-teaching.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07
